message.content:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - Insurance premium quoting and damage claims system

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Requirements (from prompt.md):

The system supports two operations: quoting a premium for a list of items, and processing damage claims against a policy.

**Quoting a premium:**
- Each main item has insurance value and base premium:
  - sword: 1000 G / 100 G base premium
  - amulet: 600 G / 60 G base premium
  - staff: 800 G / 80 G base premium
  - potion: 400 G / 40 G base premium
- Components (runes, moonstones): 250 G each / 25 G base premium each
- Building block of 3 alike components: special base premium of 60 G (instead of 75 G)

Premium modifiers:
- Cursed items: +50% risk surcharge
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5): +30% risk surcharge
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years with MHPCO): -20% loyalty discount
- First insurance: +10% initial assessment surcharge
- Each contract after the first: -15% discount
- +5 G processing fee on every premium
- All amounts rounded to whole G in MHPCO's favor (round up)

**Processing claims:**
- Deductible of 100 G per damage event
- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8: reimbursed at 50% of damage amount
- Damage to items made of dragon material: fully reimbursed
- (Otherwise, presumably no reimbursement beyond ... actually the spec says claims "may concern" these — so only these are reimbursable)

**Input/Output:** JSON scenario with customer + steps array; CLI at src/cli.ts reads JSON from stdin and writes JSON to stdout. Quote results have `premium`, claim results have `payout`.

Note: The CLI entry point will be src/cli.ts and the core logic should live in src/claim-office.ts. Tests should cover the core logic (the processScenario function or similar). A separate CLI test may be needed.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

Create a focused test list (it.todo) covering BASE functionality only, ordered simple → complex. Do not add generic "validates input types" tests. Focus on the actual business rules from the spec.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

metrics.json
node_modules

src

vitest.config.ts

1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: quoting a premium for a list of
15	items, and processing damage claims against a policy.
16	
17	### Quoting a premium
18	
19	When a customer wants to insure a list of items, the system computes the
20	premium. Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium
21	according to the MHPCO price list: a sword is insured at 1000 G with a
22	base premium of 100 G, an amulet at 600 G / 60 G, a staff at 800 G /
23	80 G, a potion at 400 G / 40 G. Components — for example runes and
24	moonstones — are insured at 250 G each, with a base premium of 25 G.
25	A building block of 3 alike components is offered at a special base
26	premium of 60 G.
27	
28	Several modifiers apply to the premium:
29	
30	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
31	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
32	  surcharge.
33	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
34	  20 % loyalty discount.
35	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
36	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
37	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
38	
39	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
40	
41	### Processing claims
42	
43	When damage occurs to insured items, the customer files a claim. A
44	deductible of 100 G applies per damage event.
45	
46	Claims may concern damage to items with high enchantment level (≥ 8) or
47	made of dragon material:
48	
49	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
50	  the damage amount.
51	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
52	
53	## Task
54	
55	Implement the two operations (quote, claim) so that they correctly model
56	the MHPCO policy behavior as described above.
57	
58	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
59	`claim-office`. The executable reads a JSON document from stdin
60	describing a sequence of operations, and writes the corresponding
61	results as a JSON document to stdout.
62	
63	### Input format
64	
65	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
66	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
67	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
68	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
69	later steps may refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote` step
70	via its zero-based step index in the `policy` field of a `claim`.
71	
72	### Output format
73	
74	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array. The `results`
75	array has the same length as the input `steps`, in the same order. Each
76	result object contains the fields appropriate to the operation:
77	
78	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
79	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G).
80	
81	### JSON Schema (normative)
82	
83	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
84	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
85	
86	**Input (stdin):**
87	
88	```json
89	{
90	  "type": "object",
91	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
92	  "properties": {
93	    "customer": {
94	      "type": "object",
95	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
96	      "properties": {
97	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
98	      }
99	    },
100	    "steps": {
101	      "type": "array",
102	      "items": {
103	        "oneOf": [
104	          {
105	            "description": "Quote step",
106	            "required": ["op", "items"],
107	            "properties": {
108	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
109	              "items": {
110	                "type": "array",
111	                "items": {
112	                  "type": "object",
113	                  "required": ["type"],
114	                  "properties": {
115	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
116	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
117	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
118	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
119	                  }
120	                }
121	              }
122	            }
123	          },
124	          {
125	            "description": "Claim step",
126	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
127	            "properties": {
128	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
129	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
130	              "incident": {
131	                "type": "object",
132	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
133	                "properties": {
134	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
135	                  "damages": {
136	                    "type": "array",
137	                    "items": {
138	                      "type": "object",
139	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
140	                      "properties": {
141	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
142	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
143	                      }
144	                    }
145	                  }
146	                }
147	              }
148	            }
149	          }
150	        ]
151	      }
152	    }
153	  }
154	}
155	```
156	
157	**Output (stdout):**
158	
159	```json
160	{
161	  "type": "object",
162	  "required": ["results"],
163	  "properties": {
164	    "results": {
165	      "type": "array",
166	      "items": {
167	        "oneOf": [
168	          {
169	            "description": "Quote result",
170	            "required": ["premium"],
171	            "properties": {
172	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
173	            }
174	          },
175	          {
176	            "description": "Claim result",
177	            "required": ["payout"],
178	            "properties": {
179	              "payout": { "type": "integer" }
180	            }
181	          }
182	        ]
183	      }
184	    }
185	  }
186	}
187	```
188	
189	### Schema example 1 — Quote only
190	
191	Stdin:
192	
193	```json
194	{
195	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 0},
196	  "steps": [
197	    {
198	      "op": "quote",
199	      "items": [
200	        {"type": "sword", "material": "steel", "enchantment": 3, "cursed": false}
201	      ]
202	    }
203	  ]
204	}
205	```
206	
207	Stdout shape:
208	
209	```json
210	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}]}
211	```
212	
213	### Schema example 2 — Quote followed by a claim
214	
215	Stdin:
216	
217	```json
218	{
219	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
220	  "steps": [
221	    {
222	      "op": "quote",
223	      "items": [
224	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
225	      ]
226	    },
227	    {
228	      "op": "claim",
229	      "policy": 0,
230	      "incident": {
231	        "cause": "fire",
232	        "damages": [
233	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
234	        ]
235	      }
236	    }
237	  ]
238	}
239	```
240	
241	Stdout shape:
242	
243	```json
244	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>}]}
245	```
246	
247	The two examples illustrate the **shape** of stdin and stdout. Concrete
248	values are not given here; deriving them from the rules above is part of
249	the task.
250	
251	## Expected Output Files
252	
253	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
254	- Test files
255	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
256	
257	## Constraints
258	
259	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
260	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
261	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
262	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
263

content.text:
Test list created at `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`.

Test List Summary:

Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - Insurance premium quoting and damage claims
Test File: `src/claim-office.spec.ts`
Implementation File: `src/claim-office.ts` (to be created)
Base Functionality Tests: 27

Test Cases (ordered simple to complex):

1. Empty scenario returns empty results
2-5. Base premiums for each main item type (sword, amulet, staff, potion) with fee + first-insurance surcharge
6-11. Component pricing: single rune, single moonstone, two alike, building block of 3 runes, building block of 3 moonstones, unlike components not combined
12. Summing multiple main items in one quote
13-16. Risk surcharges: cursed (+50%), high enchantment (+30%), enchantment 4 boundary, both surcharges combined
17-20. Customer modifiers: first-insurance (+10%), per-contract discount (-15%), loyalty (-20% at >= 2 years), no loyalty < 2 years
21-22. Processing fee (5 G) and rounding up
23-27. Claims: non-reimbursable returns 0, enchantment >= 8 gives 50%, dragon material full, single deductible per event, payout 0 when below deductible
28. End-to-end: quote followed by claim referring to that policy

Advanced Features NOT included (saved for later):
- CLI/stdin/stdout integration (separate concern in `src/cli.ts`)
- Multiple incidents/cause-specific logic
- Combinations of many modifiers across complex scenarios
- Mixed item types in a building-block context (interaction with main items)
- Input validation / error handling beyond base
- Building blocks for multiples beyond 3 (e.g., 6 alike → two blocks)

Next Step: Use the `red` agent to activate the first test ("should return empty results for scenario with no steps").
